overview: Nvidia began in 1993 when Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, and Curtis Priem bet that consumer PCs would need dedicated chips for real-time 3D graphics. The insight was right, but their first architecture was wrong: Nvidia chose quadrilaterals while Microsoft's DirectX standardized triangles, and Sega abandoned its planned console chip. With nine months of runway, Huang cut 70% of staff and used an unproven software emulator to design the Riva 128 without normal hardware iterations, compressing a two-year process into six months.

Riva's success created a rapid product cadence, a TSMC partnership, the GeForce brand, and an IPO. Nvidia then escaped pure component commoditization by inventing the GPU category and betting again on programmable shaders, letting developers write software for graphics hardware and enabling Microsoft's Xbox. Revenue surged from $158 million to $1.4 billion in three years, yet margins and growth later weakened as ATI copied features and Microsoft captured ecosystem value. By 2006, Nvidia had product-market fit and survival DNA—but still needed CUDA to create durable Power.

takeaways.title: Correct markets do not rescue wrong architectures
takeaways.detail: The founders correctly anticipated consumer 3D graphics but chose a quadrilateral primitive incompatible with the triangle standard Microsoft embedded in DirectX. Sega's withdrawal left Nvidia with obsolete work, demonstrating that technical cleverness can lose when an ecosystem converges elsewhere.

takeaways.title: Simulation turned bankruptcy into process advantage
takeaways.detail: With nine months of cash, Nvidia could not afford repeated foundry prototypes. It spent roughly one-third of its remaining money on software emulation, debugged a chip running one frame every 30 seconds, and taped out directly—creating a six-month development rhythm while competitors needed 18–24 months.

takeaways.title: Relentless cadence outran Moore's Law
takeaways.detail: Riva 128 sold one million units in four months. Nvidia then delivered roughly doubled graphics performance every six months, compared with Moore's Law's 18–24-month cadence, making organizational speed a temporary form of Process Power and attracting TSMC as a foundational manufacturing partner.

takeaways.title: Programmability made the GPU strategically independent
takeaways.detail: GeForce 3's programmable shaders shifted lighting and effects from fixed functions to developer-written code. Nvidia and Microsoft extended C into Cg, establishing the GPU as a real processor rather than a disposable peripheral and foreshadowing the proprietary software stack later embodied by CUDA.

takeaways.title: Product-market fit still lacked durable Power
takeaways.detail: By 2006 Nvidia had created a large graphics market, gone public, and reached billion-dollar revenue, but switching remained easy, competitors copied programmable shaders, and Microsoft extracted console economics. The company needed a second invention that competitors and customers could not readily reproduce.

chapters.title: Discomfort becomes Huang's operating advantage
chapters.summary: Sent alone from Taiwan to a Kentucky reform school at nine, Huang learns to navigate unfamiliar environments and tolerate adversity—traits that later define Nvidia's repeated near-death recoveries.
chapters.quote: I can tolerate a lot of discomfort

chapters.title: ASIC work teaches customer-driven chip building
chapters.summary: At LSI Logic, Huang embeds with Sun Microsystems and translates workstation requirements into silicon, gaining the product, systems, and customer experience complementing his cofounders' engineering depth.
chapters.quote: turn it into reality and production

chapters.title: Three founders choose consumer 3D graphics
chapters.summary: At a Denny's meeting, Huang, Malachowsky, and Priem decide to bring workstation-class graphics acceleration to ordinary PCs, creating the first company dedicated to consumer graphics chips.

chapters.title: A terrible pitch still earns elite backing
chapters.summary: LSI CEO Wilf Corrigan's reference to Don Valentine overcomes Huang's unfinished plan and poor presentation; Sequoia and Sutter Hill invest $2 million at a $6 million post-money valuation.
chapters.quote: Wilf says to give you money

chapters.title: DirectX invalidates Nvidia's quadrilateral bet
chapters.summary: Nvidia and Sega design around four-sided polygons, but Microsoft standardizes Windows 3D graphics on triangles; competitors adopt DirectX and Sega abandons Nvidia's NV2 for the Dreamcast.
chapters.quote: DirectX made some pretty different design decisions

chapters.title: Nine months remain to reinvent everything
chapters.summary: Facing an obsolete architecture and lost anchor customer, Nvidia lays off 70% of employees and asks the remaining 35 people to design and ship an entirely new chip before cash runs out.

chapters.title: Software emulation eliminates hardware iterations
chapters.summary: Nvidia risks a third of its cash on an unproven emulator, verifies the design at one frame per 30 seconds, and orders 100,000 units without the customary prototype-and-revision cycle.

chapters.title: Riva 128 establishes a six-month cadence
chapters.summary: The powerful but imperfect DirectX chip sells one million units in four months, while Nvidia institutionalizes a release cycle three to four times faster than competitors and gains share every generation.

chapters.title: Morris Chang answers Nvidia's handwritten appeal
chapters.summary: After Riva proves Nvidia's potential, Huang writes directly to TSMC's founder. The companies sign a multiyear foundry agreement that combines Nvidia's design velocity with leading manufacturing.
chapters.quote: Morris Chang is on the phone

chapters.title: GeForce creates and names the GPU category
chapters.summary: The GeForce 256 delivers roughly five times competing performance, and Nvidia markets it as the first GPU—elevating graphics from an add-in card toward a distinct computing processor.
chapters.quote: Before this, the term GPU didn't exist

chapters.title: Xbox finances the programmable-shader leap
chapters.summary: Microsoft advances $200 million within a $500 million annual supply agreement, while GeForce 3 and the Cg language let developers program lighting and effects directly on the GPU.
chapters.quote: This move to programmable shaders was a bet the company move

chapters.title: Product-market fit arrives before durable Power
chapters.summary: By 2006 Nvidia has created the GPU market, but switching remains easy, ATI copies programmability, Microsoft captures ecosystem economics, and the six-month process lead is narrowing—setting up CUDA as the necessary second invention.
chapters.quote: had definitely found product market fit, but... not yet... power

title: Nvidia Part I: The Near-Death Invention of the GPU
